segments.s714-g81: همی‌خورد is the classical/poetic imperfective marker همی + present stem خورد ‘eat’, here indicating a habitual or ongoing action (“was eating”); the subject بهرام follows the verb (common in older/poetic word order). و simply coordinates the next verb. بخشید is the past form of بخشیدن and here means “gave, bestowed” (not ‘forgave’); چیز is the direct object of بخشید and comes after the verb, meaning “a thing/something.”
segments.s714-g82: برو بر is a compact, somewhat formulaic sequence: برو (literal “go”) + بر (“on/upon”) functions here as a discourse connector — roughly “thereupon” or “on that” in poetic narrative — rather than a literal imperative. بسی is an intensifier meaning “much/very” (classical usage of بس/بسی), آفرین is a noun “praise/acclamation,” بود is the past copula “was,” and نیز adds “also/too.” Together they mean “thereupon there was much praise as well.”
segments.s714-g73: بر آیین چین is a prepositional phrase placed before the verb: بر (on/upon) + آیین (custom/practice) + چین (China) gives 'according to the Chinese custom'. خلعت is the direct object 'robe(s) of honour' placed before the verb in poetic word order, and آراستند is the past-tense 3rd-person-plural verb (آراستن → آراستند) meaning 'they adorned/arrayed (him)'.
segments.s714-g74: فراوان here is an adverb/modifier meaning 'plentifully/many' placed before the nouns it quantifies. کلاه و کمر is a coordinate noun phrase ('hats and belts/girdles'), and خواستند is the past 3rd-plural of خواستن meaning 'they wanted/asked (for)'; the nouns before it are its object (they asked for many hats and belts).
segments.s714-g75: جز از is an excluding prepositional phrase 'except (for)'. داد و خورد here function as a nominal pair (literally 'gave and ate') used idiomatically for what was given and consumed (gifts/provisions). شکارش is 'his prey/hunt' (شکار + possessive -ش). نبود is the past negative copula 'there was not/ he did not have'. Together: 'There was no prey of his except what was given and eaten.'
segments.s714-g76: This is a simple negative nominal clause: غم 'sorrow' + گردش روزگارش 'the turning/vicissitude of his days' (گردش modifying روزگار with the possessive -ش) + نبود 'was not'. The meaning is 'he had no sorrow over the course/turning of his days.'
segments.s714-g77: بزرگان چینی is a noun phrase 'the great ones / grandees of China' (بزرگان + adjective/attributive چینی). و simply coordinates with the following noun. گردنکشان is a descriptive plural (here 'proud ones' / 'those of high neck' — i.e. proud people); the whole phrase lists the groups being described.
segments.s714-g78: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. بهرام یل is a name plus appositive/title 'Bahram the hero' (یل in apposition to بهرام). داشتندی is the archaic past 3rd-plural form 'they had' (داشتن), and نشان is the object 'token/mark'. Word order: prepositional source 'from Bahram the hero' + verb 'they had' + object 'a token'.
segments.s714-g79: همه چین means 'all of China' (subject). همی‌گفت is the habitual/continuous past form (همی- + گفت), indicating 'kept saying' or 'would say'. ما بنده‌ایم is reported speech: present-tense copula -ایم inside the quote ('we are servants'), with بنده‌ایم used as a humble self-reference 'we are your humble servants'.
segments.s714-g80: ز بهر تو is a purpose/reason phrase 'for your sake' (ز ... بهر = 'because of/for the sake of'), اندر جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world', and زنده‌ایم is the present plural copula meaning 'we are alive / we live'. Together in the reported speech: 'For your sake we live in the world.'
segments.s714-g49: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خاتون is the noun 'the lady' (subject/topic). بشد is an archaic/poetic past-like form meaning 'it happened/it became' (here 'had/held' — roughly equivalent to modern شد in temporal clauses). دست او is the object phrase 'her hand' (دست = hand; او = her). بوس is a noun 'a kiss' and داد is the past tense verb 'gave' (i.e. 'gave a kiss' = 'kissed'); the verb comes after object phrases as normal in Persian.
segments.s714-g50: برفتند is the past plural 'they departed/rode off'. گردان is a collective noun meaning 'troop' (acting as the subject of برفتند). فرخ نژاد is a compound epithet modifying گردان: فرخ 'fortunate/auspicious' + نژاد 'born/lineage' = 'fortunate-born' or 'of auspicious lineage.' Word order is adjective(s) after the noun in many epic phrases but here read together as a unit describing the troop.
segments.s714-g51: همه 'everyone' is the subject. هم‌زبان here is an adverbial/phrase meaning 'with one voice' or 'of one tongue' (i.e. unanimously). آفرین is an exclamation/acclamation 'bravo!' and خواندند is the past plural 'they uttered/they chanted' — together meaning 'they all cried "Afarin!"'.
segments.s714-g52: ورا is the accusative/clitic-like form 'to him'. شاه ایران‌زمین is the noun phrase 'king of the land of Iran' (شاه = king; ایران‌زمین = the Iranian realm). خواندند here is used in the sense 'they called/proclaimed' (sense 2) — i.e. 'they called him king.' The verb agrees with the plural subject implied earlier.
segments.s714-g53: گرفتش is a fused past verb: گرفت (seized) + ـش (3rd-person singular object clitic 'him'). سپهدار is the agent/noun 'commander'. چین here names the region/people — 'China' — so سپهدار چین = 'the Chinese commander' or 'commander of China'. در کنار is a prepositional phrase 'to the side'/'aside' (در + کنار). The line thus reads 'the Chinese commander seized him, (and) [led him] aside.'
segments.s714-g54: وزان پس is a temporal connective: وزان ('from that') + پس ('then/after') = 'from that time on' or 'after that'. ورا again is 'him/to him' (accusative). خواندی is an archaic past form meaning 'he called' or 'he named' (third-person singular past, here used to mean 'he gave the name'). شهریار is the name/title 'Shahryar'/'king'. So the clause means 'and from then on he called him Shahryar.'
segments.s714-g55: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خاقان is the title 'khagan' (a supreme ruler) and چینی qualifies it as 'Chinese khagan'. به is the preposition 'to/toward' and ایوان is 'iwan'/'portico'; رسید is the past 'arrived/reached'. The clause therefore reads 'When the Chinese khagan reached the iwan.'
segments.s714-g56: فرستاده‌ای is a nominal phrase: فرستاده (sent one/messenger) + ـی (indefinite) = 'a certain messenger' or 'an envoy' (here functioning as direct object). مهربان is the adjective 'kind' modifying فرستاده‌ای. برگزید is the past tense 'he chose/selected'. Word order is subject (implicit), verb برگزید, and the object phrase preceding it.
segments.s714-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. پیدار شد (پیدا + شد) is a past-tense verbal phrase 'appeared' (پیدا used in the sense 'become visible' plus شد 'became'). ازآسمان is a prepositional phrase 'from the sky/heaven' and گرد ماه is a nominal phrase 'the round/moon-disk' (poetic word-order places the verb before the full subject).
segments.s714-g2: شب تیره is the subject 'the dark night'. بفشاند is a 3rd-person singular past-form used in epic narration (the b- prefix appears in classical style) meaning 'scattered' or 'sent forth'. گرد here is a noun 'dust' and سیاه modifies it 'black' — together 'scattered black dust' (i.e. a dark cloud/gloom).
segments.s714-g3: پراکنده گشتند is a past plural clause 'they were scattered/dispersed' (گشتند = 'became/turned' used intransitively). و coordinates the next clause. مستان شدند is another past plural clause 'they became drunk/ecstatic' (شدند = 'became'). The two clauses describe successive states.
segments.s714-g4: وز آنجای is an archaic sequence equivalent to 'از آنجا' meaning 'from that place' (وز here functions as a prepositional/old-form 'from'). هرکس is the subject 'each person/everyone'; به ایوان شدند is 'went to / came to the iwan' with شدند (3pl past) agreeing with the plural sense of the people. Note the poetic flexibility: هرکس is grammatically singular in some usages, but here the plural verb treats it collectively.
segments.s714-g5: چو is 'when'. پیداشد is the past 'appeared' (compound stem used as one word in poetry). آن marks 'that/the'. فرخورشید is a poetic noun 'sun-gleam / solar radiance' (subject, here immediately following the verb for stylistic inversion). زرد is the adjective 'yellow/pale' modifying it.
segments.s714-g6: به here introduces the action (roughly 'and/then' or 'upon/around') with پیچید (past 3rd sg 'twined/entwined/turned'). زلف شب لاژورد is the object: زلف 'lock(s) of hair' qualified by شب 'of night' and لاژورد 'lapis/azure' (a poetic image: the night has lapis-blue locks which are wound/entwined by the sun‑gleam). Word order places the verb before its object for poetic effect.
segments.s714-g7: قژ آگند is a compound adjective 'creak‑filled' or 'full of creaking/noise' (an onomatopoetic epithet, here describing a suit of armor or garment). پوشید is the simple past 'he put on/wore'. بهرام گرد functions as the subject (Bahrām with the epithet/part of name 'گرد'); the verb preceding the subject is a poetic inversion typical of epic style.
segments.s714-g8: گرامی تنش را is the definite object: گرامی 'dear/honored' modifying تن + the possessive clitic ش 'his' and را marking the object ('his dear body'). به یزدان سپرد is a transitive past clause 'he entrusted (it) to God' (سپرد = 'entrusted/handed over', یزدان = 'God').
segments.s714-g57: فرستاد is a simple past third-person verb 'he sent.' The noun phrase 'ده بدره گنجی درم' follows the Persian order Numeral + Noun + Modifier and describes what was sent: 'ده' (ten) quantifies the following noun cluster. گنجی modifies درم (treasure-related dirhams), so taken together the phrase means ten treasure-chests/sets of dirhams.
segments.s714-g58: همان here = 'those same' referring back to the chests just mentioned. و برده is a verbless clause where برده is best read as the past participle 'taken' (i.e., they were taken). از بیش و کم is an idiomatic expression 'with more and less,' meaning amounts varied; از links the variation to the taking.
segments.s714-g59: که introduces a content/purpose clause here. رو پیش is a compound meaning 'go before / in the presence of' (رو = toward/face + پیش = before/in presence of). بهرام is the addressee/one to be addressed. جنگی بگوی uses جنگی adjectivally with the verb بگوی (classical subjunctive/imperative form of گفتن) to mean 'speak/announce about war' or 'declare war.' The structure expresses an instruction: 'go before Bahram and say (of) war.'
segments.s714-g60: The second که begins the reported content: 'that.' نزدیک ما یافتی is past-tense 'you found/obtained near us' (یافتی = you found/obtained). آبِ روی is an idiomatic, poetic collocation: literally 'water of the face' — here meaning a fair/beautiful face or favour; together it names the fair-faced woman that was discovered near them.
segments.s714-g61: پس ('then/moreover') opens a new remark. پرده ما is a possessive noun phrase 'our curtain/veil' with the possessor after the possessed (typical poetic order). یکی دخترست is a fused copular phrase: یکی = 'a certain/one,' and دخترست = 'is a daughter' (دختر + -ست, archaic/poetic copula), yielding 'there is a daughter.'
segments.s714-g62: که is the relative 'who/that.' بر introduces the locative/figurative position 'upon.' تارک اختران is a poetic phrase meaning 'the brow/crest of the stars' (tārak here is the forehead/brow in context, though the supplied sense list does not include that sense). افسرست is a predicative compound (افسر + -ست), here 'is a diadem/helmet' used metaphorically: 'she is a diadem upon the stars' brow.'
segments.s714-g63: کنون‌گر is a poetic compound meaning 'if now' (کنون = now, گر = if). بخواهی is a present/subjunctive 'you desire/wish' used in the protasis of the conditional. ز من دخترم = 'my daughter (from me)' — ز marks source/possession in poetic diction; the phrase means 'my daughter' offered by me.
segments.s714-g64: سپارم is a first-person singular present-future/optative sense 'I will entrust / I will give.' بتو = 'to you.' لشکر و کشورم lists what will be entrusted: 'my army and my realm' (کشورم = my country/realm; the possessive -م is attached). The verb governs both objects as the things offered to the addressee.
segments.s714-g17: کمان را بمالید is a past narrative clause: کمان (direct object) is marked by the object marker را, and بمالید is a past third-person singular form of مالیدن (he rubbed). وِبر (وبر) combines و + بر = “and upon,” linking to the following prepositional idea; زه نهاد is a second past clause where نهاد is past of نهادن (he placed/put) and زه (the bowstring) is its object, so the two actions are sequential (“he rubbed the bow and placed it on the string”).
segments.s714-g18: ز یزدان here uses ز as a poetic preposition meaning “from” or “by” (origin/agent). نیکی دهش is a nominal/verb phrase meaning “granting of goodness” (دهش as ‘giving, grant’); کرد باد is an optative formula: کرد (past of ‘to do/make’) plus باد (an optative particle meaning “may it be”), a classical Persian way to express a wish (‘may [God] do/grant’). The whole clause functions as a wish: “May God grant goodness.”
segments.s714-g19: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” بر اژدها برشدی is a compact clause: بر (on/upon) + اژدها (dragon) marks the target, and برشدی is a past verb meaning ‘mounted/rose upon’ (classical past narrative). The clause sets the time/background for what follows (“when he leaped/attacked the dragon”).
segments.s714-g20: نبودی is the past negative ‘there was not’ (classical narrative). برو تیر is an anaphoric/deictic phrase referring to that particular arrow (poetically compressed here), and کس کارگر means ‘any man’s (کس) effective (کارگر)’ — together the inverted word order expresses: “there was no man’s arrow that was effective” or more naturally, “no arrow of any man proved effective” (i.e. none was more effective than his).
segments.s714-g21: شد is a past narrative auxiliary/verb ‘became/happened’ used to introduce the event. آن شیر کپی is a noun phrase ‘that lion (کپی here used as an epithet/descriptor in the poem)’, and به چشمه درون is a prepositional phrase ‘into the spring’ (به … درون = into). The clause means roughly ‘that lion entered into the spring.’
segments.s714-g22: This segment strings three past verbs with و (and) to show successive actions: به غلتید (he rolled/tumbled into/there), و برخاست (and rose up), و آمد برون (and came out). Each verb is simple past third-person; و links them as a narrative sequence, and برون marks the exit ‘out.’
segments.s714-g23: بغرید is past third-person ‘it roared.’ و links actions. بر زد is ‘struck/struck at’ (past). The poetic inversion places بران سنگ دست (lit. ‘upon-that rock hand’) where a prose order would be دست بر آن سنگ زد — i.e. ‘it struck that rock with its hand/paw.’ The segment therefore describes a roar followed by a blow against the rock.
segments.s714-g24: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle often meaning ‘indeed/so/then’ (here rendering immediacy). آتش (fire) is the subject; از marks origin (‘from’); کوه خارا is ‘the flinty/rocky mountain’ (خارا used as a rock/flint descriptor); بجست is a past verb meaning ‘leapt’ or ‘sprang forth.’ Together: ‘indeed fire leapt from the flinty mountain.’
segments.s714-g9: This is a coordinated noun phrase with a past-tense verb implied: 'کمند و کمان برد' functions as 'he took/removed the lasso and the bow.' Word order: direct objects (کمند, کمان) come before the verb برد (classical past of بردن 'to take/carry'), then another coordinated object phrase follows (و شش چوبه تیر = 'and six shafts/shafts of arrows'). The conjunction و links items in a list.
segments.s714-g10: This is a short enumerative noun phrase: یکی (numeral 'one') quantifies نیزه ('spear'), followed by a modifier 'دو شاخ' ('two‑horned' / 'two‑pronged') that describes نخچیرگیر. نخچیرگیر here names a hunting implement or device ('game‑catcher'/'hunter') and stands in apposition or as a compound description of the spear.
segments.s714-g11: چوآمد is a fused classical form meaning 'when he came' (چو + آمد). The following prepositional phrase به نزدیک آن برزکوه places the action spatially: به (towards/into) + نزدیک ('near') + آن ('that') + برزکوه (proper name 'Barzkuh'). The clause as a whole is temporal/subordinate ('when he came near that Barzkuh').
segments.s714-g12: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 'he commanded/ordered.' تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that' / 'that'), and بازگردد is the present-subjunctive/infinitival idea 'should return' / 'return.' گروه is the object 'the group/band/troop' which is being ordered to return. Word order: verb (honorific) + subordinating تا + verb phrase + object.
segments.s714-g13: بران is an adverbial/discourse particle 'thereupon' / 'on that (account)'. شیر is the noun 'lion' and کپی here functions as a name or epithet attached to شیر (its exact lexical sense is unclear in the list, so it is treated as a proper/name). چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when', and نزدیک شد is the verbal phrase 'became near' / 'drew near' (نزدیک + شد 'became'). The segment sets up a temporal clause.
segments.s714-g14: This is direct speech introduced in narrative: تو (you) + گفتی (you said — here reported speech; the provided sense list lacks the verb sense, so treated as the speech verb) followed by the imperative برو ('go'). The clause 'کوه تاریک شد' follows as the content of what was said: کوه (mountain) + تاریک (dark) + شد (became) — 'the mountain became dark.' Word order: subject of speech + speech-verb + imperative + independent clause conveying the reported utterance.
segments.s714-g15: میان اندارن is a prepositional phrase 'between them' (میان = between / middle, اندارن = 'in them' / 'among them'), followed by the noun phrase کوه خارا ('the flinty / hard mountain' — خارا modifying کوه). ببست is the past tense 'he closed / he bound / he blocked.' Together the clause means he blocked/shut the flinty mountain between them (i.e., closed a passage).
segments.s714-g16: بخم is an archaic/obscure adverbial particle here (sense labelled 'obscure/archaic') marking a transition (often 'then' or similar). The phrase کمند از بر زین نشست parses as: کمند (the lasso/snare) + از (from) + بر (on/upon) + زین (saddle) + نشست (sat / settled / was set). In context this describes the lasso being placed/hung from the saddle or otherwise set with respect to the saddle; نشست is used in the sense 'sat/settled' (intransitive) of the item or actor.
segments.s714-g41: سر از تن جدا کند is a verbal phrase meaning 'to separate the head from the body.' Persian orders the direct object (سر) before the prepositional complement (از تن) here: literally 'head from body separate (he) does.' کند (3sg of کردن) and بفگند (3sg with the prefix be-) are narrative verbs in the same clause and should be read as successive actions — 'he cuts off ... and he throws (it).' خوار is an adjectival/adverbial qualifier meaning 'despised/treated with contempt' modifying how it was thrown. The verbs are classical/narrative forms (historic/historical present usage typical of epic Persian).
segments.s714-g42: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial 'thereupon / after that.' فرود آمد is a compound verbal idea: فرود (descent/landing) + آمد (came) = 'he descended.' از کوهسار marks the source/place 'from the mountain.' Word order is adverbial — verb — source, a normal narrative sequence.
segments.s714-g43: ازان بیشه is a locative phrase 'from that grove.' The agents خاقان و خاتون (the khaghan and the lady) are the subject, and برفت is the past narrative verb 'departed / went away' (classical past form). The prepositional phrase precedes the subject for emphasis; the verb concludes the clause, as is common in Persian narrative.
segments.s714-g44: دمان و دنان form a paired locative phrase describing slopes/skirts of the land (poetic/archaic words). تا introduces the extent 'as far as / up to,' and برکوه تفت names the place 'on Mount Taft' (بر- + کوه = 'on the mountain' plus the proper name تفت). Several words here are archaic or proper names: دنان and تفت are poetic/placename items whose modern dictionary senses do not appear in the provided list.
segments.s714-g45: خروشی (a roar/clamour) is the subject noun and برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth.' ز گردان چین is a genitive/agentive phrase 'from the assembled troop/rows' (گردان = troop, چین = folds/rows/formation) indicating the source of the sound. Word order is noun + verb + source-prepositional phrase.
segments.s714-g46: کز introduces a relative/adverbial clause 'from which / by which.' آواز is 'sound' or 'voice.' گفتی here is an archaic/simulative particle meaning 'as if / one would say' (not the modern noun sense 'conversation'); it creates the sense 'a sound as if ...'. بلرزد is subjunctive/optative-style present 'would tremble' or a literary present used to indicate potential effect, and زمین is the object 'the earth.' The whole clause characterises the sound as so powerful that 'one would say the earth trembles.'
segments.s714-g47: به بهرام is a dative phrase 'to/for Bahram.' برآفرین functions as an exclamatory epithet ('bravo' / an applause-form) addressed to Bahram. خواندند is the past plural verb 'they called/sang/shouted,' so the clause means 'they cried out “Bravo to Bahram!”'.
segments.s714-g48: بسی here is a poetic adverb meaning 'many/much' (used to qualify the quantity), followed by the nouns گوهر و زر 'jewels and gold.' برافشاندند is past plural 'they scattered / they showered (down),' with the prefix بر- intensifying the action; together the clause says they showered many jewels and gold.
segments.s714-g65: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' (indirect object). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here used narratively; the provided lemma senses list does not include the simple past meaning). بهرام is the subject 'Bahrām' placed after the verb (Persian permits subject after verb). کاری رواست is a short predication: کاری 'a deed/action' + رواست (=روا + است) 'is fitting/permissible', where رواست is a poetic/contracted form of 'رواست' 'it is fitting'.
segments.s714-g66: جهاندار is the subject 'the world‑ruler/sovereign'. بر is the preposition 'over/against' governing بندگان 'subjects/servants' (here meaning people under rule). پادشاست is پادشاه + است compressed into one word meaning 'is king' (predicate nominal), so the clause reads 'the sovereign is king over his subjects.'
segments.s714-g67: This is a compact past-tense clause in which به + بهرام marks the indirect object 'to Bahram', داد is the past verb 'gave', آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time/then', and دخترش is the direct object 'his daughter' (دختر + enclitic possessive -ش). Word order is flexible: the indirect object and temporal phrase appear before the verb.
segments.s714-g68: به فرمان او is an ablative/agentive phrase 'by his command' (به here marked as 'by/through'), شد is the past of شدن 'became' (meaning 'came to be' or 'was made so'), and همه کشورش is a noun phrase 'all his country/whole realm' (همه = whole/all, کشور + ش = his realm). Together: 'by his command his whole realm came to be [under him].'
segments.s714-g69: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/said'. تا here functions narratively as 'and then' / marking the result/time, introducing the following clause. پیش او شد دبیر is literally 'the scribe came before him' with دبیر 'scribe/secretary' as subject placed after شد ('came'/'arrived'), and پیش او = 'in his presence'.
segments.s714-g70: نوشتند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they wrote/inscribed' (implying the scribes). منشور نو is a noun phrase 'a new charter' (منشور 'charter', نو 'new'). بر حریر is a prepositional phrase 'on silk' (بر = on/upon, حریر = silk). The clause states the action of inscribing the new charter on silk.
segments.s714-g71: بدو گفت is again 'he said to him' (بدو = to him, گفت = said). هرکس کز ایران سرست is a relative clause: هرکس 'whoever', کز (archaic contraction of 'که از') 'who/that from', ایران = 'Iran/Iranians', and سرست = سر + ست (سر 'head/leading' + ست = 'is'), so 'whoever from Iran is foremost/heads (the group)'.
segments.s714-g72: ببخشش is ببخش + ش (the verb 'give/grant' or 'forgive' with the 3rd‑person object enclitic -ش), so here 'grant it to him' / 'give to him'. نگر is an imperative 'look/consider' (a command to examine). تا introduces purpose/result 'so that', کرا is an archaic interrogative/relative 'who/whom', در خورست is a predicate meaning 'is fitting/proper' (در = in; خورست is an older/poetic form meaning 'is appropriate' — the provided lexical senses do not match perfectly). The whole instruction: grant to deserving Iranians and examine to whom each thing properly belongs.
segments.s714-g25: Indirect (poetic) word order: the object کمان + object marker را appears before the verb بمالید (past, 3rd sg.). بمالید here is the simple past of مالیدن 'to rub/handle' and is used idiomatically for preparing/handling the bow. The following اسمِ فاعل phrase بهرام گرد is the subject placed after the verb for stylistic effect (بهرام = the hero; گرد here functions as an epithet/part of his name in the line).
segments.s714-g26: به تیر is instrumental/means 'with/by an arrow' (به used as 'by/with'); از هوا is a source phrase 'from the air'; روشنایی is the object 'light/brightness'; ببرد is the past-subjunctive/preterite form of بردن used here in the sense 'to take/carry' — together: 'with an arrow he took light from the air.' The clause is compact and slightly telegraphic as is common in epic style.
segments.s714-g27: خدنگی is the direct object 'a dagger'; بینداخت is past tense 'threw/ cast' (3rd sg.). شیر دلیر is the subject phrase 'the brave lion' placed after the verb for poetic inversion; دلیر is an adjective modifying شیر.
segments.s714-g28: This clause is elliptic and somewhat compressed: برشیر functions as a locative/target 'upon/on the lion'; کپی شد is a predicative phrase 'became/was made like a copy' (شد = 'became'); از جنگ سیر is an ablative + adjective phrase 'sated/of war' meaning 'weary or sated from battle.' Together the clause conveys that, with respect to the lion, something was made or became as if a copy — i.e., an effect on the lion, with سیر qualifying the weariness from جنگ. (The phrasing is terse and metaphorical, typical of the epic register.)
segments.s714-g29: دگر here means 'another' (an additional one) and modifies تیر; the verb زد is past 'he struck/shot.' Word order places the actor بهرام after the noun phrase (poetic inversion): literally 'another arrow Bahram struck on his head.' بر + سرش = 'upon his head' where -ش is the 3rd‑person possessive enclitic.
segments.s714-g30: فرو ریخت is a two-word verbal phrase meaning 'poured down' (فرو 'down(wards)' + ریخت, here with the verbal sense 'to pour/flow'); چون introduces a simile 'like/as'; آب خون = 'water of blood' i.e. 'blood like water'; ازبرش in this context is an adverbial/ablative indicating source 'from his side/ from him' (the enclitic -ش marks possession). The whole clause: 'blood poured down like water from him.'
segments.s714-g31: Numeral + noun: سیوم تیر (the third) and چارم (the fourth) are ordinal references to successive arrows; the coordinating و links them. بزد is past 'he struck/smote'; بر دهانش 'upon his mouth' where -ش is the 3rd‑person possessive. The order places the numerals and the object before the verb, a typical poetic arrangement.
segments.s714-g32: که introduces a result/nominal clause 'so that / so that it happened that'; بردوخت is a compound reading here (بر دوخت) meaning 'was sewn/stitched together' (the participial/passive sense 'stitched'); برهم functions adverbially 'together/one upon another'; دهان و زبانش = 'his mouth and (his) tongue' (ش is the 3rd‑person enclitic on زبان implied for the second coordinated noun). The clause states the violent result: his mouth and tongue were sewn up.
segments.s714-g33: به پنجم is an adverbial/time phrase placed before the verb meaning "on the fifth (shot/time)"; بزد is the simple past 3rd-person singular "he struck/shot" and takes an object. تیر is the direct object "an arrow"; بر marks the target/against/onto, and چنگ اوی is a noun + 3rd-person possessive enclitic (میان/after-noun clitic) meaning "his claw/hand". Word order is typical older Persian: time phrase — verb — object — prepositional target.
segments.s714-g34: همی‌دید is a present/imperfective or iterative past sense here translated as "he perceived/kept seeing"; it takes a direct object. نیروی is a noun "force/strength" and is coordinated with آهنگ. و is the simple coordinator "and." آهنگ here means "intention/aim/intent" rather than melody; اوی is the 3rd-person possessive "his." The clause states what he perceived about the addressee/foe.
segments.s714-g35: میانش is the noun میان "middle" plus the enclitic -ش meaning "his/its" ("his middle"); گشاد is a predicate adjective meaning "wide/gaped/open." از کمند is a prepositional phrase giving the source/agent of the opening ("from the snare"). The front element بهشتم functions as an adverbial/time/manner marker in this poetic line (placed before the clause) and is archaic/poetic in shape here.
segments.s714-g36: بجست is a past-tense 3rd-person singular verb here meaning "he leapt/sprang." از بر is a fixed-ish locative combination meaning "from the side of/from off" and is followed by کوهسار بلند, a noun phrase "the high/lofty mountainside." Word order places the verb before the adpositional phrase in this short poetic clause.
segments.s714-g37: بزد is the past 3rd-person singular "he struck/struck at." نیزه‌ای is the indefinite object (نیزه + -ای) meaning "a spear." بر marks the target ("at/into") and میان دده is a noun phrase "the middle/midriff of the dede/creature" (میان + possessive/name). The clause is a straightforward transitive past construction: verb + (indefinite) object + prepositional target.
segments.s714-g38: که is a relative/connecting particle "which/that" referring back to the struck place; شد is the past of "to become/happen." سنگ خارا is an adjectival noun phrase "flinty/hard stone" (سنگ + خارا), and به خون آژده is an adjectival/participial phrase meaning "(made) with blood/covered in blood" — here functioning to describe the state resulting from the blow. The whole clause is a result clause: "which became ..."
segments.s714-g39: وزان پس is a temporal/discourse sequence marker: وزان ("and from that/thereupon") + پس ("then/afterwards"). بشمشیر is an instrumental nominal form meaning "with the sword" (بـ- prefix + شمشیر); یازید is an older/poetic verb meaning here "opened/split (it)" or "made a gash," and مرد is the subject "the man/hero." The line uses a compact, somewhat inverted order typical of epic poetry: temporal marker — instrument phrase — verb — subject.
segments.s714-g40: تن اژدها را is the direct object phrase (تن "body" + اژدها "dragon" + را the definite object marker). به دونیم کرد is a resultive/causative phrase: به + دونیم ("into two halves / in two") and کرد (past of کردن "to do/make") — literally "made (it) into two." Together: "he made the dragon's body into two (pieces)."

sentences.s714-s41: Bahrām was eating and gave away a thing; thereupon there was much praise as well.
sentences.s714-s37: They, following the Chinese custom, arrayed him in robes and asked for many hats and belts.
sentences.s714-s38: He had no prey except what was given and eaten; he had no sorrow at the vicissitudes of his days.
sentences.s714-s39: Chinese grandees and proud ones bore a token from Bahram the hero.
sentences.s714-s40: All China kept saying, 'We are your servants; for your sake we live in the world.'
sentences.s714-s25: When the lady offered her hand he kissed it; the fortunate-born troop departed.
sentences.s714-s26: All, with one voice, cried 'Bravo!' and they proclaimed him king of the land of Iran.
sentences.s714-s27: The commander of China seized him and drew him aside; from that time on he called him Shahryar.
sentences.s714-s28: When the Chinese khagan reached the iwan, he chose a kind envoy.
sentences.s714-s1: When the round moon appeared from the sky, the dark night scattered black dust.
sentences.s714-s2: They were dispersed and became intoxicated; from that place each person went to the iwan.
sentences.s714-s3: When that yellow sun-gleam appeared, it wound the night's lapis-blue locks.
sentences.s714-s4: Bahrām Gerd put on creak‑filled (armor) and entrusted his noble body to God.
sentences.s714-s29: He sent ten chests of treasure — dirhams; those same chests were taken, some with more and some with less.
sentences.s714-s30: Go before Bahram and speak of war, saying that you have found a fair-faced one near us.
sentences.s714-s31: Moreover, behind our curtain there is a daughter who is a diadem upon the brow of the stars.
sentences.s714-s32: Now, if you wish, I will give my daughter to you and entrust to you my army and my realm.
sentences.s714-s9: He rubbed the bow and set it on the string; may God grant goodness.
sentences.s714-s10: When he sprang upon the dragon, there was no man’s arrow more effective than that one.
sentences.s714-s11: That lion-like beast went into the spring; it rolled, rose up, and came out.
sentences.s714-s12: It roared and smote that rock with its paw; fire leapt from the flinty mountain.
sentences.s714-s5: He took the lasso and the bow and six arrow-shafts; one spear, two-pronged, a game-catcher.
sentences.s714-s6: When he came near that Barzkuh, he ordered the band to return.
sentences.s714-s7: When the lion Kapi drew near, you said, 'Go — the mountain has grown dark.'
sentences.s714-s8: He closed the flinty mountain between them; then he hung the lasso from the saddle.
sentences.s714-s21: He severed the head from the body and cast it away in contempt; after that he descended from the mountain.
sentences.s714-s22: From that grove the khaghan and the lady departed, from the skirts and slopes up to Mount Taft.
sentences.s714-s23: A roar arose from the ranks of the host, from whose sound one would say the earth trembles.
sentences.s714-s24: They shouted 'Bravo to Bahram!' and showered many jewels and gold.
sentences.s714-s33: Bahrām said to him, “This deed is fitting — the sovereign is king over his subjects.”
sentences.s714-s34: At that time he gave his daughter to Bahram; by his command his whole realm came under him.
sentences.s714-s35: He commanded; then the scribe came before him, and they wrote a new charter on silk.
sentences.s714-s36: He said to him, 'Give to whoever among the Iranians is foremost; look, so that you see to whom (each thing) properly belongs.'
sentences.s714-s13: Bahram the champion drew his bow; with an arrow he took brightness from the air.
sentences.s714-s14: The brave lion hurled a dagger; upon the lion (he) became like a copy, sated from battle.
sentences.s714-s15: Bahram struck another arrow into his head; blood poured down from his side like water.
sentences.s714-s16: He struck the third and the fourth arrows at his mouth, so that his mouth and tongue were sewn together.
sentences.s714-s17: On the fifth he shot an arrow at his claw; he perceived his strength and his intent.
sentences.s714-s18: The snare gaped open at his middle, and he sprang from the lofty mountainside.
sentences.s714-s19: He struck a spear into the creature's midriff, which became like flint-stone, stained with blood.
sentences.s714-s20: After that he drew his sword; the man cut the dragon's body in two.
